article_title: UK Government Abandons AI Training Copyright Exception After 88 Percent of Consultation Respondents Backed Mandatory Licensing

content_preview.title: UK Government Abandons AI Training Copyright Exception After 88 Percent of Consultation Respondents Backed Mandatory Licensing
content_preview.summary: The UK government published its statutory report on copyright and AI on March 18, dropping its preferred opt-out exception for AI training after overwhelming opposition and signaling a shift toward market-led licensing and transparency obligations.
content_preview.body_excerpt: The UK government on March 18 published a [125-page report and economic impact assessment on the use of copyright works in AI development](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/report-and-impact-assessment-on-copyright-and-artificial-intelligence), fulfilling a statutory deadline set by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 and confirming a significant policy reversal: it will no longer pursue a broad copyright exception that would have allowed AI developers to train on protected works unless ...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt
1 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

editor_notes.source_verification: Two gov.uk sources verified directly: the 125-page report (published March 18, 2026) and the statement of progress both confirm 88% support for licensing, 3% for Option 3, four working groups with 50+ members, and the market-led approach. The Parliament source (publications.parliament.uk) returned HTTP 403 due to bot blocking — this is a known UK parliamentary website behavior and the domain is authoritative.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Key claims cross-checked: 11,500+ consultation submissions, 88% for licensing, Option 3 abandoned, £124 billion creative industries contribution, four working groups. All confirmed via government sources.
editor_notes.editorial_quality: Well-structured News article at 748 words (within 400-1200 range). Clear section headings, balanced coverage of creative industry and tech perspectives. No editorializing or sensationalism. Neutral tone maintained throughout.
editor_notes.verdict_rationale: APPROVE. All substantive claims verified against primary government sources. The parliamentary source 403 is a known bot-blocking pattern for an authoritative domain. Article provides thorough, balanced coverage of a significant UK policy development.
